---
title: "Optic Neuritis and Transverse Myelitis"
---

OPTIC NEURITIS
Epi: usually 20s-40s, 2:1 F:M
Presentation: monocular vison loss/scotoma, red desat, painful eye movements (90%), Uhtoffâ€™s
Exam: swollen disks in 1/3 only, central vision loss, poor color plates/red hatpin, +APD
Workup + Admission + Treatment:
1) Blood tests, CXR, and LP are not helpful (unless atypical ON); order MRI for prediction of MS
2) Consider formal VF testing (Goldmann or Humphrey). Consider NMO testing since treatment of this
condition is different from routine optic neuritis.
3) Admit for IV Solumedrol 1g/d x 3-5 days, expect recovery in 2-3 weeks
4) Plan to start immunomodulatory therapy if MRI with >2 lesions (>3mm diam, perivent or ovoid) to reduce
risk of MS: Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) group had 44% less risk of CDMS at 3 years (CHAMPS trial, Jacobs
et al, 2000); interferon beta-1a (Rebif) group had 34% probability of conversion to MS at 2 years (compared to
45% of placebo; ETOMs trial, Comi et al, 2001); interferon beta-1b group had 28% probability of conversion
to MS at 2 years (compared to 45% of placebo; BENEFIT trial, Kappos et al, 2006)
Evidence for treatment in acute optic neuritis (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial, 1992,1993,1997)
ï‚· IV steroids - speed vision recovery, no change in acuity vs. placebo at 5 yrs
ï‚· IV steroids - 50% less develop CDMS at 2 yrs (8% vs 17%), but no difference at 5 years
ï‚· Oral steroids - increased recurrent ON vs IV/placebo (41% vs 25%) over 5 yrs (donâ€™t treat acute ON with
oral steroids)
ACUTE TRANSVERSE MYELITIS
1. Demyelinating:
NMOSD:
Dx Criteria: Core Clinical Characteristics: Optic neuritis, acute myelitis, Area postrema syndrome, Acute
Brainstem syndrome, Acute diencephalic syndrome
Additional MRI requirements: for ON: Optic Nerve MRI inflammation >1/2 optic nerve. For myelitis: cord lesion
> 3 segments, MRI brain not c/w MS, NMO-IgG positive (sen 58-76%, sp 85-99%)
NMOSD with AQP4-IgG: At least 1 core clinical; Positive AQP4-IgG
NMOSD with negative AQP-IgG: At least 2 core clinical (at least 1 has to be ON or TM); Dissemination in
space; MRI requirements as applicable
* Distinguish from MS: long cord lesions, usually nl MRI brain (atypical when they occur), CSF can have cells
(PMN), neg CSF OCBs, NMO Abs rare in MS
Treatment: Pheresis (acute); Rituximab (prophylaxis) at 375mg/m2 weekly x 4 weeks (Wingerchuk, et al
Neurology 2015:85:1-13)
2. Infectious: Dx Testing should include HSV-1,2; EBV, VZV, lyme, HTLV-1, HHV-6, CMV, WNV
3. Misc inflammatory: SLE, Sjogrenâ€™s, MCTD, Bechetâ€™s, Sarcoidosis
4. Nutritional: B12, folate, copper, vitamin E
Approach:
1. MRI GAD ïƒ  R/o Compression (if compression, call a surgeon)
2. If > 3 segments, consider NMO, SLE, Cu def, sarcoid, infarcts, infection
* If pleocytosis (> 20-50 cells), check HSV, VZV, Lyme, EBV, HHV-6, HTLV-1, Enterovirus, WNV
* Serologic testing should include: ANA, SSA/B, Lyme, Anti-cardiolipin Ab & Anti-phopholipid Abs, HTLV-1,
HIV-1, RPR, B12, folate, Copper
* More ancillary testing: Chest CT (sarcoid), Lip Biopsy (Sjogrens), Brain/Meningeal Biopsy
CSF inflammation and its mimics = inflammogram
ï‚· CSF: oligoclonal bands, myelin basic protein, VDRL, JC virus PCR (if immunosuppressed)
ï‚· Serum: SPEP, ANA, ANCA, SSA/B, ACE, RPR, Lyme ab, HIV, B12, ESR, TSH
